title: Anger As Information: Listening To Your Body’s Messages About Work
dek: You’ve been taught that fury is ugly, but what if your anger is not a flaw but a map? When your jaw clenches and your diaphragm tightens at your desk, your body is speaking in muscle and fascia. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your anger holds precise, somatic information about your relationship with your work, if you learn to listen.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Jaw Remembers: How Anger Lives in Your Body

You’ve probably noticed it before—how the tightness sets into your jaw like a clenched fist, long before you’ve said the word that unleashes the anger. It’s there in the masseter muscles, those thick bands of flesh running from your cheekbone to your lower jaw, hardening as though braced against some unseen pressure. Maybe it’s late afternoon, the office window throwing a pale winter light across your desk, and your breath feels shallower, the corners of your mouth pulling taut. Your body is speaking a language you were never taught to listen to: the jaw remembers.

This isn’t some vague metaphor; it’s biology. The temporomandibular joint, where your jaw hinges, carries the memory of every suppressed growl and swallowed retort. The fascia surrounding these muscles tightens, gripping like a net, holding onto the tension you weren’t allowed to express. You might catch the faint metallic taste of stress on your tongue, or the dry heat pooling behind your ears, where the superficial temporal artery pulses with quiet insistence. When you rest your hand along your jawline, you can feel the difference between relaxed flesh and the rigid, unyielding clench that settles in after a day spent biting back what you really want to say.

I think of my teacher, Dr. Yu, who first pointed out that the jaw is a common seat of anger trapped in the body. She taught me to place my fingers gently on the masseter, to breathe into the tightness, and to acknowledge the story held in that muscle—stories of being told your fury was ugly, unworthy, or dangerous. The jaw doesn’t just hold physical tension; it holds the echo of those messages. When you finally allow that muscle to soften, it’s as if a lock clicks open on a storied chest you didn’t realize you were carrying around.

Sometimes, the tension extends down into the sternocleidomastoid muscles, the thick cords running along the sides of your neck, stiff as wire. You might notice the skin there feels clammy, and a cool breeze slipping through an open window makes you shiver because your body is on edge, braced for impact. This is your nervous system responding to anger it wasn’t permitted to express. It stores this fury in the body like a ledger, each muscle and fascia layer a line item in a long overdue accounting.

You don’t have to push through this tension or numb it out with caffeine or sugar or endless busywork. Your jaw remembers because it needs you to listen. The body knows what the mind often refuses to say. When you place your hands on your face at the end of that long, frustrating day, you might notice the heat, the tightness, the subtle tremble just beneath the skin. These are not signs of weakness; they are the body’s way of holding a truth that demands your attention.

Anger, when held in the jaw, is not pathology. It’s biology—muscles and fascia responding to the stories you’ve been told and the moments you weren’t allowed to speak. The ache behind your ears, the tension in your neck, the clench of your jaw—they are all messages. Not to be feared or dismissed but to be heard. Your body remembers. When you start to listen, you begin to reclaim what was denied: the right to feel your fury without shame.

## The Diaphragm and Breath: Tensions That Tell Stories

You sit at your desk, the late afternoon sun slanting through the window, and you notice the way your breath feels shallow, almost as if it’s been clipped at the bottom. The diaphragm—the muscle beneath your lungs, domed like a tiny parachute—should be moving deeply and rhythmically, but instead, it’s tight and stubborn. That tension, lodged beneath your ribs, is not random; it’s a message encoded in your body’s oldest language. The diaphragm remembers what you’ve been holding in, even if your mind has forgotten.

When you’re angry at work but have learned to squash that fury, your diaphragm braces itself. It pulls taut, like a spring wound too tight, pressing against the bottom ribs and the fascia around the kidneys. This isn’t pathology; this is biology telling you there’s something to attend to. The tightening of the crura of the diaphragm—the tendinous legs that wrap around the aorta and esophagus—compresses not only your breath but your ability to fully digest the emotional weight you carry.

Feel into the subtle tension sitting in the lateral edges of your diaphragm, those thin muscular slips attaching to the inner surfaces of your lower ribs. Often, they harden in response to stress, reducing the ease of expansion when you inhale. This constriction cuts off the flow of oxygen to your brain and muscles, making frustration feel like a physical throb in your temples or a gnawing ache between your shoulder blades where the scalenes and levator scapulae clench. The jaw remembers this too: the masseter and temporalis muscles tense, grinding teeth into a clenched line as if to hold your anger in place.

You might notice this tension most clearly in the quiet moments—the stillness just before sleep when the world has softened and the night’s cool air smells faintly of rain and earth. Lying on your back, you can trace the subtle rise and fall of your belly and chest, feeling the diaphragm’s labor. When it’s tight, your breath can feel punctuated, as if you’re trying to inhale through a narrow straw. Your ribs don’t flare open; your belly doesn’t soften. This is the body’s way of guarding itself, a clenched fortress built from years of unheard frustration.

Teachers like Donna Farhi remind us that breath is the thread between body and mind, that to listen to its rhythm is to listen to yourself. The diaphragm is not just a muscle but a storyteller of your internal landscape. When it’s constricted, your body is telling you there’s anger, and not the kind that needs to be controlled or denied, but the kind that wants to be witnessed and understood.

Herbs like Pleurisy root, used traditionally to support lung and diaphragm health, can ease the fascial tightness around this muscle, but the real release comes when you allow the breath to expand fully—when you give permission to the diaphragm to descend as it was designed. You might try a long exhale on a cool evening, the air thick with the scent of wet leaves, focusing on the subtle muscles between your ribs—the intercostals—and the sheath of connective tissue that can hold onto tension like a whispered secret.

This is not a call to fix or force, but to listen—to the body that knows, the diaphragm that tightens in response to the stories your workday leaves behind. Your breath carries those stories; your body waits for you to hear them.

## Historical Lessons: Cultures That Honored Fury in Work

You might notice the tight coil of your temporalis muscle—right there above your ear—throbbing as you read these words. That’s anger, a visceral heat that can flare without apology, and for most of us, it’s been a flame we were taught to smother. But there are histories where fury was not the enemy of work but its fierce ally, a vital current pumping through the sinews of labor and justice.

Consider the Indigenous women of the Pacific Northwest, whose salmon harvests required more than quiet patience. Their anger was a communal force against overfishing and environmental harm, a sharp edge cutting through complacency. They didn’t tuck away their fury behind polite smiles; it was carried in the set of the trapezius muscle, the way their shoulders squared against injustice, the way their voices rose like the tide in council fires. This was not petulance or pathology but a deliberate, embodied resistance to harm.

Or look to the ancient Greek muses of work—not the poetic inspirations floating in the ether, but the real, sweat-drenched workers whose fury fueled revolts and reforms. In the agora, the muscles of their forearms, knotted from weaving or smithing, spoke a language of demand. They knew the jaw remembers—the habitual clench during moments when words failed or when a task demanded relentless effort. Anger was the pulse beneath the skin, a signal that the work was not just labor but a claim on respect and fairness.

Robert Augustus Masters, a teacher who doesn’t sanitize anger into something palatable, insists that fury is information lodged deep in the body’s fascial networks. He writes of anger as a messenger that you must listen to with somatic attentiveness—not as a problem to be fixed, but as a guide to the boundaries your body insists on maintaining. His insight unearths the truth that the body knows before the mind can name it: anger arises in the psoas when you feel trapped, in the clenching of the masseter when your voice is unheard, in the heat behind the sternum when your worth is questioned. Masters teaches that honoring this anger means engaging the muscles and fascia in a dialogue, releasing their tension through movement and breath, rather than forcing them into silence.

In the drumbeats of West African women’s labor songs, fury marked the rhythm of resistance and endurance. Their bodies, slick with the humid sweat of midday work under the sun, flexed and stretched with intention. The serratus anterior and rhomboids held stories of struggle, and the sharp intake of breath before a chorus was a shared acknowledgment that anger was not an interruption but the very engine of perseverance.

So, when you feel your own fury rising—perhaps in the tight squeeze of your biceps or the sharp pinch in your lower trapezius—remember these histories. Your anger is not a flaw but a lineage, a deep muscle memory passed through generations who understood that fury in work is a herald. It’s the body speaking, demanding that you listen with the same fierce respect that those women and workers did before you. This is not pathology; this is biology, the body’s way of saying, “Something here needs to change.”

## Listening to the Fascia: What Tightness Reveals

It’s late afternoon, and you’re sitting at your desk, the cold light of a rainy Thursday filtering through the window. Your shoulders creep toward your ears, the trapezius muscle shriveling into itself like a tightly wound spring. Underneath, the fascia—the dense, web-like connective tissue that envelops your muscles—feels rigid, like someone has wrapped your upper back in invisible cords. This is not just tension; it’s a message. The fascia remembers every dismissal, every moment you swallowed your fury to keep the peace, every time you told yourself to “just get through it.” The tightness is the body’s way of whispering what your mind is too busy to hear.

Fascia is often overlooked because it doesn’t contract like muscle, but it holds your history—your emotional and physical biography—like a second skin. When you clench your jaw at 3 a.m., grinding your molars into the night’s quiet, the masseter muscle tightens, but it’s the fascia beneath that hardens like a shield. This isn’t pathology; it’s biology. Your body is archiving the moments you were told your anger was ugly or unworthy. The sternocleidomastoid, that long neck muscle running from your collarbone to behind your ear, tenses when you’re trying to hold your head high while swallowing a bitter pill at work. The fascia around it thickens, less fluid, less forgiving.

I remember a teaching from Tom Myers, who says fascia is the body’s continuous fabric, a living matrix that both supports and restricts. When your workday leaves you feeling squeezed and small, your thoracolumbar fascia—the sheet of connective tissue in your lower back—may tighten, binding your ribs and pelvis in a silent, aching embrace. This tightness doesn’t just hurt; it tells you where you are holding back, where your fury is boxed in, where the body is trying to protect your fragile edges from fracturing under pressure.

There’s a deep, almost medicinal smell in the air of your skin when fascia tightens—slightly sour, a mingling of sweat and the subtle musk of lactic acid buildup. It’s not pleasant, but it’s honest. It’s your body saying, “I’m holding this for you.” When you recognize this, you can respond. A poultice of crushed fresh comfrey leaves, applied to the areas where fascia is knotted tight, can soften that webbing over days. Comfrey’s allantoin-rich leaves nourish fascia and muscle, coaxing them back toward fluidity and ease. You can find a ready-made salve like [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20) that respects this ancient healing without diluting it with trendy fluff.

Your body knows exactly where your fury lives. It’s not hiding in your head or swirling in vague emotions; it’s packed into the fascia, clenched in the splenius capitis at the base of your skull, tied tight through the rhomboids between your shoulder blades, locked in the psoas deep in your pelvis. When you listen to these tissues, when you trace the tightness with your breath and your fingers, you begin to understand your anger not as something to be fixed or silenced, but as vital information. This is where somatic wisdom lives—raw, unvarnished, waiting for you to acknowledge it.

## Herbs That Support the Body’s Processing of Anger

You might notice how the tension in your masseter muscle—the one that clenches along your jawline, right under your earlobe—tightens as you replay that meeting where your voice was drowned out. That’s the jaw remembering. It’s not pathology; it’s the body’s way of storing information, signaling that your boundaries were pushed. When you bring herbs into this somatic conversation, you’re not numbing the feeling but offering your tissues a chance to breathe, to metabolize the fire in a way that respects its power.

Karla McLaren’s work comes alive here: anger is not a flailing enemy but a messenger, a finely tuned alarm system that tells you where your limits lie and where your energy wants to move. She reminds us that anger’s rawness is part of its clarity, and your body’s sensations are the language it uses. Your liver fascia, that slick sheath that wraps and supports the organ responsible for detoxification and energy flow, responds to emotional stagnation. When anger gets stuck, the liver’s smooth, sliding motion tightens, and you feel it as a dull ache under the right ribcage or a knot in your upper trapezius. The herbs you choose become allies in this internal dialogue, encouraging the fascia to soften and the muscles to release without forcing or bypassing the feeling.

Take Bupleurum (Chai Hu), a root known in Chinese medicine for its ability to soothe what’s called “Liver Qi stagnation.” When you sip a gently brewed decoction in the late afternoon, as the sun softens and the air cools, it carries a bitter, slightly warm energy that helps smooth your internal currents. This isn’t sedation; it’s a working-through. The bitterness wakes up the liver’s ability to process not just toxins but emotional charge. You might feel a loosening in the upper trapezius or a shift in your breathing pattern—deeper, more expansive—as the fascia unglues itself from the ribs.

Then there’s Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca), a fierce, spiky plant that tastes intensely bitter and smells faintly of damp earth. It’s specific for the heart that is racing with unexpressed fury. When your chest feels tight, when the pectoralis minor pulls your shoulders forward in a protective hunch, Motherwort acts as a steady hand on your back. It doesn’t erase the anger; it gives your heart the capacity to hold it without breaking rhythm. A few drops of the tincture under your tongue can shift the somatic landscape, allowing the diaphragm to drop and the breath to widen.

And we cannot forget Dandelion root (Taraxacum officinale). Often dismissed as a weed, it is a profound ally for the liver and gallbladder, the organs most intimately connected to anger in TCM. When you roast the root and drink it as a dark, earthy brew, it stimulates bile flow, which in turn helps digest the heavy, fatty emotions that sit like stones in your gut. The body knows how to process anger when given the right tools. Dandelion reminds your tissues that even the most stubborn roots can be transformed into nourishment.

These herbs are serious medicine. They demand respect and attention. When you use them, you are not trying to fix a broken system; you are supporting a biological process. You are telling your body, “I hear the anger in the jaw, the tightness in the liver, the racing in the heart, and I am here to help you move it.”

## Somatic Practices to Engage with Work-Related Fury

When the 3 p.m. slump hits and your inbox is a minefield of passive-aggressive requests, you might feel the familiar heat rising in your chest. Your jaw remembers the last time you had to swallow your words, the masseter muscles tightening like a vice. This isn’t a flaw in your character; it’s your body’s brilliant, biological response to crossed boundaries. The fascia around your neck and shoulders—specifically the levator scapulae—pulls taut, bracing for impact. Your body is holding the fury you aren’t allowed to express in the boardroom.

To engage with this anger, you must first locate it. Sit in your chair and bring your attention to the physical sensations. Notice the shallow, clipped rhythm of your breath as your diaphragm refuses to descend fully. Feel the subtle, sour smell of stress sweat that might be prickling at your hairline. This is the somatic reality of your anger. It’s not an abstract concept; it’s a physiological event happening right now in your tissues.

Here is a short embodied practice to help you move this energy without suppressing it. Plant your feet firmly on the floor, feeling the solid ground beneath your shoes. Place one hand on your belly and the other on your chest. Inhale deeply, allowing your belly to push into your hand, bypassing the tight chest breathing that anxiety demands. As you exhale, make a low, guttural sound—a hum or a growl—letting the vibration travel up from your diaphragm, through your throat, and out of your mouth. Feel the vibration in your jaw, allowing the masseter to soften slightly with the sound. Repeat this three times. You are not trying to get rid of the anger; you are giving it a channel to move through your body.

Bessel van der Kolk’s research underscores this necessity: the body keeps the score, and trauma—even the micro-traumas of a toxic workplace—lodges in our physiology. He emphasizes that cognitive understanding is rarely enough to shift deep-seated somatic patterns. When you try to rationalize your anger away, your body remains locked in a state of hyperarousal. The psoas muscle, deep in your core, stays contracted, ready to fight or flee, even while you sit perfectly still at your desk. Engaging with your fury means speaking the body’s language of movement, breath, and sensation, rather than relying solely on the mind’s language of logic.

Another practice involves the hands. When anger flares, your hands often want to curl into fists. Let them. Clench your fists as tightly as you can, feeling the tension travel up your forearms to your biceps. Hold this contraction for a count of five, acknowledging the raw power in your grip. Then, slowly, deliberately, release the tension. Spread your fingers wide, feeling the rush of blood returning to your palms. Notice the tingling sensation, the sudden warmth. This intentional contraction and release teaches your nervous system that it can hold intense energy and then let it go safely.

You don’t have to fear your fury. It is not a monster to be slain but a messenger to be heard. When you engage with it somatically, you honor the wisdom of your tissues. You acknowledge that the tightness in your jaw and the heat in your chest are valid responses to your environment. By moving this energy through your body, you reclaim your power, transforming work-related anger from a silent burden into a dynamic force for clarity and boundary-setting.

## Navigating Anger Without Shame or Suppression

The fluorescent lights hum above your desk, casting a sickly pallor over the afternoon, and you feel it again—the familiar, sudden heat pooling at the base of your skull. Your splenius capitis tightens, pulling your head back slightly as if bracing for a blow. This is your anger, raw and unvarnished, rising in response to a boundary crossed or a voice ignored. For years, you were taught to swallow this heat, to force a smile while your masseter muscles ground your teeth into a silent, aching powder. But this suppression is not a virtue; it is a violence against your own biology.

When you push anger down, it doesn’t vanish. It settles into the fascia, the connective tissue that wraps every muscle and organ, turning it rigid and unyielding. You might notice the smell of your own sweat changing—sharper, more acrid—when you force yourself to be pleasant in the face of disrespect. Your body knows the truth. The jaw remembers the words you didn’t say, and the psoas, deep in your pelvis, contracts as if preparing to flee a danger you refuse to acknowledge. This is not pathology; this is your nervous system doing exactly what it was designed to do.

Resmaa Menakem teaches that trauma and unexpressed emotion live in the body, specifically in the vagus nerve and the deep tissues. He emphasizes that we cannot simply think our way out of these somatic holding patterns. When you feel the surge of anger at work, your body is mobilizing energy for self-protection. If you suppress it, that energy turns inward, creating a toxic internal environment. Menakem’s work reminds us that healing requires us to settle our bodies, to notice the constriction without judgment, and to allow the energy to move through us rather than locking it away in our muscles.

To navigate this anger without shame, you must first stop apologizing for it. When the heat rises, don’t immediately try to cool it down. Feel the tension in your trapezius, the way your shoulders creep toward your ears. Acknowledge the tightness in your diaphragm that makes your breath shallow and clipped. This is your body communicating vital information about your environment. It is telling you that something is wrong, that a line has been crossed, that your integrity is demanding to be upheld.

Instead of suppressing the fury, try to track it. Notice where it lives in your body. Does it sit heavy in your chest, pressing against your sternum? Does it flutter in your solar plexus? When you locate the physical sensation, you can begin to work with it. You might excuse yourself to the restroom, run cold water over your wrists, and take three slow, deep breaths, focusing on expanding your ribcage laterally. This doesn’t erase the anger, but it signals to your nervous system that you are present and capable of handling the intensity.

You can also use herbs to support this process. A strong infusion of nettle leaf, drunk cool throughout the day, provides deep nourishment to a nervous system frayed by constant suppression. Nettle is a fierce plant, covered in stinging hairs, yet it offers profound grounding and mineral support. It reminds your body that it is possible to be both fiercely protected and deeply nourished.

Navigating anger is not about explosive outbursts or silent seething. It is about listening to the body’s messages with respect and curiosity. It is about recognizing that your fury is not a flaw, but a powerful, biological ally designed to keep you safe and whole. When you stop shaming your anger, you start listening to your body, and that is where true somatic wisdom begins.

## When Anger Becomes Guide and When It Needs Support

The clock on your computer reads 4:17 p.m., and the sky outside is the bruised purple of an impending storm. You feel the familiar knot tightening in your solar plexus, right where the diaphragm meets the ribs. This isn’t just stress; it’s anger, sharp and insistent. Your body is speaking, and the message is clear: a boundary has been breached, a value compromised. When you learn to listen, this fury becomes a guide, a visceral compass pointing toward what must change in your work life.

Your jaw remembers the times you’ve ignored this compass. The masseter muscles clench, the fascia around your temporomandibular joint thickens, and the dull ache behind your ears becomes a constant companion. This is the body’s way of holding the line when your mind refuses to. Anger, in its purest form, is a biological imperative for self-preservation. It tells you when to say no, when to demand more, and when to walk away. It is the heat that fuels the courage to speak up in a meeting or to negotiate a fairer contract.

But there are times when this anger, left unprocessed, becomes a stagnant pool rather than a flowing river. When the fury no longer moves you to action but instead keeps you awake at 2 a.m., your heart hammering against your ribs and your sheets damp with sour-smelling sweat, it needs support. The body knows when it is overwhelmed. The tension shifts from a temporary bracing to a chronic holding pattern. Your levator scapulae—the muscles that lift your shoulder blades—become hard as stone, and your breath remains perpetually shallow, trapped in the upper chest.

This is when you must turn to somatic support, not to silence the anger, but to help the body metabolize it. You cannot simply think your way out of a clenched psoas or a tight diaphragm. The body requires physical intervention to release the trapped energy. This might look like vigorous movement—stomping your feet, shaking your arms, or pressing your hands firmly against a wall until your muscles tremble. It might look like deep, intentional vocalization, letting a low growl vibrate through your chest and throat, loosening the constricted fascia.

Herbal allies are also crucial here. When anger turns to chronic irritability and the liver feels hot and congested, a tincture of Oregon Grape root can offer profound support. Its bitter, cooling properties help to clear the stagnant heat, encouraging the liver to process not just physical toxins, but emotional ones as well. You take it not to numb the fury, but to give your body the capacity to digest it.

You must also recognize when the anger is pointing to a situation that cannot be salvaged. If your body is constantly in a state of hyperarousal, if your jaw is always clenched and your digestion is perpetually knotted, the anger is telling you that the environment itself is toxic. No amount of somatic tracking or herbal support can replace the necessity of leaving a harmful situation.

Your anger is not a pathology to be cured; it is biology to be understood. It is a fierce, protective force that demands your attention and respect. You don’t have to fear your fury. You can learn to ride its currents, to let it be your guide when it’s alive and cutting through fog, and to offer it balm when it’s a weight you’ve carried too long.

## FAQ: Common Questions About Anger, Work, and the Body

**Q:** Why does my jaw clench so hard during work stress, and how can I soften that tension?

**A:** The masseter muscle—the one that anchors your jaw to your cheekbones—tightens like a vice when your body perceives threat or frustration. This isn’t just metaphorical; it’s fascia and muscle fibers contracting, locking your mouth in a silent scream or a grinding press. That clench is your body’s way of bracing against something it can’t yet name or control. You might notice this especially in the late afternoon, around 3 or 4 p.m., when the day’s demands have worn you down and your shoulders creep up towards your ears. To soften it, don’t just tell yourself to “relax.” Instead, place your fingertips just below your cheekbones, feel the tautness of the masseter, and intentionally drop your jaw an extra millimeter. Breathe here, feeling the stretch along the lateral fascia of the face. You can follow this with a tincture of passionflower or California poppy, herbs that ease the nervous system without dulling your edges. The jaw remembers—it stores more than you realize—but it can also unlearn that tightness.

**Q:** Sometimes my anger feels like heat rising up my neck and burning behind my eyes. What’s happening in my body?

**A:** That heat is real, a cascade of blood flow and nerve firing through the sternocleidomastoid muscles wrapping around your neck and the temporalis muscles on the sides of your head. Your body is mobilizing energy—adrenaline and cortisol swirling through your bloodstream—preparing you to act, to defend, to claim space. When you’re sitting at your desk on a humid Tuesday afternoon, and the sky outside is thick with storm clouds, you might notice the skin on your neck prickling with sweat and the temples throbbing in sync with your pulse. This isn’t pathology; it’s biology. Your nervous system is signaling something important. You might respond by taking a brief walk outside, feeling the cool air brush over your skin and slow that fiery surge. A cup of chrysanthemum tea can cool the blood and calm the heat, but don’t rush to suppress the sensation. It’s your body’s way of marking territory, of saying, “I notice this, and I need you to, too.”

**Q:** I’m afraid if I express anger at work, I’ll be labeled “difficult” or “unprofessional.” How can I honor my body’s messages without risking my job?

**A:** This is the tightrope walk you didn’t sign up for, and the body knows it. Your trapezius and rhomboid muscles in your upper back tighten as you hold your breath, swallowing your truth. The tension might even settle in your diaphragm, making the act of breathing feel shallow, clipped. Honoring your body’s messages doesn’t mean you have to shout or storm out of the room. It can start with naming the feeling quietly to yourself, feeling the heat in your sternum or the flutter in your solar plexus. Then, find a way to move that energy—maybe a brisk walk around the block, a few rounds of neck rolls, or pressing a warm compress infused with ginger and black pepper along your neck and shoulders. When you’re ready, practice setting clear boundaries in small ways: a calm, firm “I need time to consider this” or “I want to revisit this conversation tomorrow.” Your body remembers these acts of self-respect, and over time, your nervous system shifts from bracing to balancing.

**Q:** How can herbs support me when my work-related anger feels overwhelming and persistent?

**A:** Herbs are not a quick fix or a way to numb out anger; they are allies in the slow work of listening to and changing your body’s conversation with itself. When your anger feels like a constant drumbeat in your solar plexus—aching in the rectus abdominis and tightening the linea alba—herbs like St. John’s Wort or Skullcap can gently ease the nervous system’s overdrive. If your anger curls into anxiety, knotting the iliopsoas muscles deep in your pelvis, a decoction of valerian root combined with chamomile can help untangle that gripping fear beneath the fury. It’s essential to work with herbs as medicine, in measured doses, respecting their power and your body’s signals. And remember, the anger that comes from work isn’t a flaw or a defect; it’s information. These herbs can help you hear that message more clearly, not silence it.
